title: Markets, property rights, and the law of demand
description: Practice what you have learned so far in this lesson. Topics include the role of property rights in markets, the income effect, and the substitution effect.
topic_path: Markets, property rights, and the law of demand

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is necessary for the effective functioning of a market system?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: In order to be willing to buy or sell something, you must be confident that a transaction cannot be undone against your will. For example, you wouldn't want to buy a carrot if you knew someone can come along and take you carrot without penalty.

choices.content: well-defined system of property rights
central control of resources of production
weak enforcement of ownership claims
prohibitions on transferring ownership
centrally coordinated prices

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is NOT a likely consequence of weakly established or absent property rights?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: In order to be willing to buy or sell something, you must be confident that a transaction cannot be undone against your will. For example, you wouldn't want to buy a carrot if you knew someone can come along and take you carrot without penalty.

choices.content: innovation
overuse of a resource
pollution
prices that do not reflect the true value and cost of something
uncertainty about ownership claims

assessment_items.question_content:
**What are the elements of property rights that help markets function?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: exclusivity, enforceability, and transferability
non-rivalry and non-excludability
durability, portability, divisibility
central planning of resource use, central planning of allocation of goods, centrally set prices
rivalry and non-excludability

assessment_items.question_content:
The government of a small town is concerned about the litter created when people leave styrofoam cups used to serve large beverages on the ground. The government decides to tax these by implementing a \$1 tax on these drinks.

**What is the most likely purpose of this tax?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Reduce the number of beverages sold in styrofoam cups
Raise tax revenue for a new park
Reward sellers of this good
Encourage people to throw the cups away
Increase the demand for these drinks

assessment_items.question_content:
**All of the following influence whether a consumer buys more of a good EXCEPT:**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: how much the consumer previously spent on the good
whether or not it is legal to buy more of the good
a change in the good's price
how much income the consumer has to spend
how much other goods cost

assessment_items.question_content:
A government is concerned about whether young children in a community are receiving enough healthcare. 

**Which of the following is the LEAST likely to increase the amount of healthcare that people provide their children?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: increase the price of healthcare
subsidize healthcare
reduce the distance traveled to get healthcare
Lowering regulations on who can provide healthcare, such as allowing nurses to provide healthcare rather than just doctors
Provide coupons for free healthcare

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes the law of demand?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: When price decreases, the quantity demanded increases.
People demand the same amount of a good no matter its price.
Legal authorities regulate prices.
Sellers set the price that demanders pay.
Demand decreases for a normal good when incomes increase.

assessment_items.question_content:
Owen saw that the price of peaches had gone down, and so he decided to buy more peaches. 

**Based on this information only, what does Owen's reaction reflect?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Owen's demand for peaches has increased.
Owen's quantity demanded for peaches has increased.
Owen has experienced an increase in income.
Owen observed that there has been an increase in the price of other fruit, a substitute for peaches.
Owen expects future price changes.

assessment_items.question_content:
**According to the law of demand, what happens when the price of a robotic sandwich stuffer decreases?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The quantity demanded of robotic sandwich stuffers increases.
The demand for robotic sandwich stuffers decreases
The supply of robotic sandwich stuffers decreases
The demand for the stuffing used by sandwich stuffers decreases
The quantity supplied of robotic sandwich stuffers increases

assessment_items.question_content:
Jeff is a rational consumer who spends his entire income on food and vacations. The table below describes his marginal utility and the prices of food and vacations.

Price of food | $MU_{food}$ | Price of vacations | $MU_{vacations}$
-|-|-|- 
$\$5$ | $20$ | $\$500$ | $2{,}000$

**If the price of a vacation decreases to $\$300$, which of the following best describes the *income effect* of this price change?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: He now effectively has more disposable income, which he can spend on more vacations, more food, or both.
The price of a vacation relative to food has decreased, so he will buy more vacations.
The marginal utility per dollar spent on vacations is lower, so he will increase how many vacations he purchases.
He will purchase less food because the marginal utility per dollar spent on food has decreased.
He will purchase more of both goods because his total income has increased.

assessment_items.question_content:
Justin is a rational consumer who spends his entire income on guitar strings and jasmine tea, both of which he considers normal goods. The table below describes his marginal utility and the prices of guitar strings and jasmine tea.

$P_{GS}$ | $MU_{GS}$ | $P_{JT}$ | $MU_{JT}$
-|-|-|- 
$\$7$ | $70$ | $\$1$ | $10$

**If the price of guitar strings decreases to $\$2$, which of the following best describes how the *substitution effect* will impact how he allocates his budget?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Justin's purchases of guitar strings have a bigger impact on his marginal utility per dollar that he spends on jasmine tea, so he will definitely buy more guitar strings.
The relative prices of guitar strings and jasmine tea have changed, guitar strings becoming relatively cheaper. He will now purchase more of the relatively cheaper guitar strings.
Justin now has more disposable income to spend on both guitar strings and jasmine tea.
Guitar strings still cost more than jasmine tea, so he will buy more jasmine tea
The price of both goods has effectively decreased, so he buys more of both goods.

assessment_items.question_content:
Emily is a rational consumer who gets utility from socks and music lessons, and she considers both of these goods normal goods. Her marginal utility from socks is $50$ utils and the price of socks is $\$5$ per pair. Her marginal utility from music lessons is $600$ utils and the price of music lessons is $\$60$

**If the price of music lessons falls to $\$20$, which of the following best describes the income and substitution effects?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The income effect is that she now can spend more on both goods. The substitution effect is that the price of music lessons relative to socks has decreased so she will definitely buy more music lessons.

The substitution effect is that she now can spend more on either good. The income effect is that the price of music lessons relative to socks has decreased so she will definitely buy more music lessons.
The income effect is that she will no longer buy socks because she has more income to spend on music lessons, so she will only buy music lessons.
The income effect is that she effectively has more money to spend on either good. The substitition effect is that she will only buy socks now.
The income effect is that she got a raise from her employer. The substiution effect is that music lessons are now relatively cheaper so she will buy more music lessons.

assessment_items.question_content:
Assume that good A and good B are substitutes for each other. Andrea is currently consuming the utility-maximizing combination of these two goods.

**How will the $MU_A$ and $MU_B$ change if Andrea alters her consumption as a result of an increase in the price of good $B$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: $MU_B$ will increase and $MU_A$ will decrease as the consumer reallocates consumption.
$MU_A$ will increase and $MU_B$ will decrease as the consumer reallocates consumption.
$MU_A$ and $MU_B$ will both definitely increase.
$MU_A$ and $MU_B$ will both definitely decrease.
$MU$ will both increase, but the $MU_A$ will increase by more than the increase in $MU_B$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Dave, Brian, and Mike's demand schedules for chicken dinners are all given in the table below. Assume that these three people are the only buyers in the market for chicken dinners.

Price | Dave's quantity demanded | Brian's quantity demanded | Mike's quantity demanded
-|-|-|-
$\$0$ | 10 | 8 | 6
$\$1$ | 8 | 6 | 4
$\$2$ | 6 | 4 | 2
$\$3$ | 4 | 2 | 0
$\$4$ | 2 | 0 | 0
$\$6$| 0 | 0| 0

**Which of the following best describes the market demand schedule for chicken dinners?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

Holly, Stephanie, and Shaun are buyers in the market for jalapeno-cheese hushpuppies. Each of their demand schedules for an order of hushpuppies is given in the table below. Assume that these three people are the only buyers in the market for hushpuppies.

Price | Holly's quantity demanded | Stephanie's quantity demanded | Shaun's quantity demanded
-|-|-|-
$\$0$ | 5 | 4 | 3
$\$1$ | 4 | 3 | 2
$\$2$ | 3 | 2 | 1
$\$3$ | 2 | 2 | 0
$\$4$ | 1 | 0 | 0
$\$6$| 0 | 0| 0

**Which of the following best describes the market demand schedule for hushpuppies?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
